subtopic_detail: Acute otitis media in Aboriginal child
stem:
A 22-month-old Aboriginal girl is brought to your remote clinic by her mother with 2 days of fever and pulling at her right ear. Her mother reports she has been waking and crying at night and is feeding less. She is fully immunised, attends day care, and has had two previous similar episodes in the last six months. There is no ear discharge.

On examination she is irritable but consolable, and there is no neck stiffness or rash. The right tympanic membrane is bulging and opaque with no perforation; the left tympanic membrane appears normal.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 28, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 38.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Right ear: bulging opaque tympanic membrane, no discharge. Left ear normal. Throat injected without exudate. No cervical lymphadenopathy. Chest clear.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Withhold antibiotics and review in 48 hours with safety-net advice
options.rationale: Watchful waiting is the default for non-severe acute otitis media in low-risk children, but Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children have substantially higher rates of suppurative complications and chronic perforation, so eTG recommends prompt antibiotics rather than delayed prescribing in this group. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Prescribe oral amoxicillin 30 mg/kg twice daily for five days for this patient
options.rationale: First-line oral amoxicillin at the higher 30 mg/kg twice-daily dose for five days is the eTG-recommended regimen for acute otitis media in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children because of higher rates of chronic suppurative otitis media and pneumococcal disease. The dose covers intermediately penicillin-resistant pneumococci. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Prescribe oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 22.5 mg/kg twice daily for seven days
options.rationale: Amoxicillin-clavulanate is reserved for treatment failure after 48 to 72 hours of amoxicillin, recurrent acute otitis media within 30 days, or concurrent purulent conjunctivitis. It is not first-line. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Refer urgently to ENT for myringotomy and grommet insertion as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Grommets are considered after recurrent acute otitis media (three episodes in six months or four in a year) or chronic otitis media with effusion causing hearing loss greater than three months, not at the acute presentation. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Prescribe intramuscular benzathine benzylpenicillin 50 mg/kg as a single dose
options.rationale: Single-dose IM benzathine benzylpenicillin is used in remote settings for streptococcal pharyngitis prevention of rheumatic fever, not as first-line acute otitis media therapy where oral amoxicillin is preferred. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Acute otitis media in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children carries a much higher risk of suppurative complications and chronic perforation than in non-Indigenous children. eTG departs from the watchful-waiting default and recommends prompt high-dose oral amoxicillin. Amoxicillin-clavulanate is for treatment failure or recurrent disease. Grommets and IM benzathine are not first-line acute treatments.
key_points: eTG: Aboriginal child with AOM gets antibiotics without delay
Amoxicillin 30 mg/kg BD for 5 days first-line
Step up to amoxicillin-clavulanate if no response at 48-72 hours
Grommets for recurrent disease or persistent effusion with hearing loss
explanation.pearls: Higher 30 mg/kg twice-daily amoxicillin dose covers intermediately penicillin-resistant Streptococcus pneumoniae and is the AU standard for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander children.

stem:
An 18-month-old girl is brought to your GP clinic by her father for a routine check. He reports she is well, walks confidently and has a vocabulary of about 30 words. She has been drinking around 1 litre of cow's milk daily since 12 months and is a fussy eater who refuses red meat. She was breastfed exclusively until six months.

On examination she is pale around the mouth and conjunctivae but otherwise well, with normal growth percentiles. There is no organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, or rash. Routine FBC is sent at the father's request.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 110, BP 92/56 mmHg, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival pallor, no glossitis, no koilonychia, no organomegaly. Tone and tendon reflexes normal. Heart sounds dual with no murmur. Chest clear.
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 88 g/L (110-140), MCV 64 fL (70-86), MCH 22 pg (24-30), platelets 460 x10^9/L (150-450), white cell count 9.2 x10^9/L (5-15), reticulocytes 0.6%; ferritin pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence oral elemental iron 3 mg/kg daily, reduce cow's milk to 500 mL daily, repeat FBC in 4 weeks
options.rationale: RCH recommends oral elemental iron 3-6 mg/kg/day for established iron deficiency anaemia in a toddler with classic dietary drivers, with concurrent reduction of cow's milk to under 500 mL daily because excess cow's milk both displaces iron-rich foods and causes occult enteric blood loss. Reassess at 4 weeks for haemoglobin rise of at least 10 g/L. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Refer for upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and faecal occult blood testing as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Endoscopy is not indicated when the dietary history and clinical picture explain the iron deficiency. Investigation for occult blood loss is reserved for failure to respond to iron replacement after 4-6 weeks of adherence, or atypical features such as failure to thrive or overt bleeding. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange haemoglobin electrophoresis and send blood film before commencing any therapy
options.rationale: Thalassaemia trait is in the microcytic differential but is usually distinguished from iron deficiency by the Mentzer index (MCV/RBC), family history, ethnicity and ferritin. A clear nutritional history with low ferritin warrants empirical iron trial first; electrophoresis is reserved for cases with normal ferritin and persistent microcytosis. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange intravenous iron carboxymaltose infusion as a single dose as the proposed approach
options.rationale: IV iron in children is reserved for malabsorption, intolerance of oral iron, or inflammatory bowel disease with ongoing losses. Oral iron is first-line in nutritional iron deficiency, with response expected by 4 weeks. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Recommend dietary modification alone and review with repeat FBC in 12 weeks as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Dietary modification alone is insufficient once iron deficiency anaemia is established because the deficit requires replacement that cannot be met by diet within a reasonable timeframe. Oral iron supplementation plus dietary change is the standard approach. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: Microcytic anaemia in a toddler with high cow's milk intake and fussy eating is overwhelmingly nutritional iron deficiency. Oral elemental iron at 3-6 mg/kg/day plus reduction of cow's milk to under 500 mL/day is first-line, with FBC review at 4 weeks. Endoscopy and electrophoresis are reserved for non-responders or atypical pictures. IV iron is reserved for malabsorption or intolerance.
key_points: Iron 3-6 mg/kg/day oral elemental iron is RCH-recommended dose
Cap cow's milk at 500 mL/day to reduce displacement and occult loss
Reassess at 4 weeks; expect Hb rise ≥10 g/L
IV iron only for malabsorption or oral failure
explanation.pearls: Excess cow's milk above 500 mL/day in toddlers causes both iron displacement and microscopic enteric blood loss; capping intake is therapeutic, not just preventive.

stem:
A 7-month-old girl has been an inpatient for 30 hours with RSV bronchiolitis. She required nasal prong oxygen 1 L/min for the first 12 hours, weaning to room air overnight. She is taking 70% of her usual feed volumes by bottle, with normal wet nappies. Her parents are reliable and live a 15-minute drive from the hospital.

On morning round she is alert and playful between feeds. There is mild subcostal recession but no head bobbing, grunting or nasal flaring. Her parents ask when she can go home.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 142, BP 92/54 mmHg, RR 44, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 36.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild subcostal recession, scattered fine crackles bilaterally, no wheeze, hydration adequate, capillary refill under 2 seconds

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Arrange overnight pulse oximetry monitoring at home through community paediatric outreach
options.rationale: Home pulse oximetry is not routinely used after bronchiolitis discharge in Australia, and the criteria for discharge in this child are already met without it. The RCH pathway uses clinical and feeding criteria, not home monitoring. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Continue inpatient observation until respiratory rate is below 40 breaths/min
options.rationale: Respiratory rate is not a discharge criterion in the RCH bronchiolitis pathway; persistent tachypnoea is expected for several days post-infection and does not preclude discharge if work of breathing is mild and feeding is adequate. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 3 days before discharge as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Corticosteroids do not alter the course of bronchiolitis in children under 12 months and are not part of the RCH bronchiolitis pathway. They have no role in discharge planning. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Discharge home now with safety-net advice and routine GP review in 1-2 days
options.rationale: RCH discharge criteria for bronchiolitis are met: SpO2 sustained at or above 92% on room air for at least 4 hours including a sleep period, taking at least 50% of feeds, mild or absent work of breathing, and reliable parents able to return. Routine GP follow-up at 1-2 days completes the safety net. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Repeat chest X-ray and capillary blood gas before deciding on discharge
options.rationale: Routine repeat imaging or blood gas is not recommended in clinically improving bronchiolitis. Imaging at any stage is reserved for unexpected deterioration or diagnostic uncertainty. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: RCH bronchiolitis discharge requires SpO2 at or above 92% on room air for at least 4 hours including a sleep period, mild or absent work of breathing, feeding at or above 50% of normal, and reliable carers with safety-net access. Respiratory rate is not a discharge criterion. Steroids, repeat imaging, and home pulse oximetry are not used. Routine GP review at 1-2 days completes the plan.
key_points: SpO2 ≥92% RA for 4 hours including sleep is the RCH oxygenation criterion
Feeding ≥50% of normal is the hydration criterion
Tachypnoea persists for days and is not a discharge criterion
No role for steroids, repeat CXR, or home oximetry
explanation.pearls: RCH bronchiolitis discharge criteria are clinical: SpO2 92% on RA across a sleep period plus feeding at half of normal plus mild work of breathing plus reliable carers.

stem:
A 14-month-old boy is admitted on day 6 of fever to 39.5 °C unresponsive to paracetamol and ibuprofen. He has been irritable and off feeds. His mother reports a non-pruritic rash that appeared on day 3 and faded, and his lips are now red and cracked. He has not had conjunctival injection or lymphadenopathy noted on previous reviews.

On examination today his lips are red and fissured with a strawberry tongue. There is firm dorsal hand and foot oedema with peeling skin at the fingertips. He has no neck node enlargement and no rash currently. He has been started on empirical IV flucloxacillin and ceftriaxone for possible staphylococcal toxic shock.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 162, BP 88/52 mmHg, RR 36, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 39.4 °C
data_table.Examination: Red fissured lips, strawberry tongue, BCG scar erythema, peripheral oedema and periungual desquamation, no conjunctival injection, no cervical lymphadenopathy
data_table.Investigations: Haemoglobin 96 g/L (110-140), white cell count 22 x10^9/L (5-15), platelets 740 x10^9/L (150-450), CRP 152 mg/L (<5), ESR 78 mm/h, ALT 88 U/L (<40), albumin 28 g/L (35-50), sodium 131 mmol/L (135-145), urinalysis sterile pyuria
data_table.Medications: Paracetamol, ibuprofen, IV flucloxacillin, IV ceftriaxone

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue empirical antibiotics for a further 48 hours before reassessing as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Persistent fever beyond 5 days with mucocutaneous changes and a markedly inflammatory profile should redirect away from staphylococcal toxic shock toward Kawasaki disease. Delay risks coronary artery aneurysm. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Refer for percutaneous coronary angiography to evaluate for coronary involvement
options.rationale: Echocardiography is the first-line imaging for coronary surveillance in Kawasaki disease in children, with high sensitivity for proximal coronary aneurysms. Invasive angiography has no first-line role. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Commence IV methylprednisolone 30 mg/kg pulse therapy as initial monotherapy
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are added in IVIG-resistant Kawasaki or risk-stratified primary therapy, but the standard initial treatment is IVIG plus aspirin. Steroid monotherapy is not used at presentation in AU practice. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange lumbar puncture to exclude bacterial meningitis before further therapy
options.rationale: LP is not indicated in the absence of meningism, altered consciousness or seizure, and would delay urgent IVIG. Aseptic meningitis can occur in Kawasaki but is a supportive feature, not an indication for LP. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange transthoracic echocardiogram and commence IV immunoglobulin 2 g/kg with high-dose aspirin
options.rationale: This child meets American Heart Association incomplete Kawasaki criteria (fever ≥5 days plus two principal features - rash and oral changes - plus supportive labs: CRP ≥30, anaemia for age, platelets ≥450, albumin ≤30, ALT elevated, sterile pyuria). Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg over 10-12 hours plus aspirin to reduce coronary artery aneurysm risk; echocardiogram baselines coronary anatomy. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: Incomplete Kawasaki disease is diagnosed when fever lasts at least 5 days with fewer than four classical criteria but supportive laboratory abnormalities (CRP, anaemia, thrombocytosis, hypoalbuminaemia, transaminitis, sterile pyuria) are present. Treatment is IVIG 2 g/kg plus aspirin, with baseline echocardiography. Delaying treatment past day 10 increases coronary aneurysm risk substantially. Steroids, LP, and angiography are not first-line.
key_points: Fever ≥5 days + 2 features + lab criteria = incomplete KD by AHA
IVIG 2 g/kg + aspirin within first 10 days reduces aneurysm risk
Echocardiogram is the imaging modality of choice
BCG scar erythema is a supportive AU-specific feature
explanation.pearls: Treating Kawasaki within 10 days of fever onset cuts coronary aneurysm rate from about 25% to under 5%. BCG scar erythema is a useful local feature in AU practice.

stem:
A 5-month-old boy presents to your paediatric assessment unit with 24 hours of fever to 39 °C, irritability and reduced feeds. A clean-catch urine grows pure Escherichia coli at greater than 10^8 cfu/L sensitive to all tested agents. He responds well to IV gentamicin and benzylpenicillin within 24 hours and is afebrile by hour 36. His antenatal scans were reported as normal at his birth hospital in regional NSW.

On examination today he is well-perfused, feeding fully and playful. There is no abdominal mass, no spinal anomalies, and his external genitalia are normal. Renal function and electrolytes have normalised.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132, BP 96/58 mmHg, RR 32, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no palpable bladder, normal external genitalia, no spinal dimple or hair tuft
data_table.Investigations: Urine MC&S Escherichia coli >10^8 cfu/L pansusceptible; sodium 138 mmol/L (135-145), creatinine 28 micromol/L, CRP peak 88 mg/L now 22 mg/L, blood cultures negative
data_table.Medications: IV gentamicin, IV benzylpenicillin (now switching to oral cefalexin to complete 7 days)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate imaging plan?

options.text: No imaging required as the UTI was typical with a good antibiotic response
options.rationale: Although a typical first UTI in a child older than 6 months does not require routine ultrasound, this child is under 6 months which independently triggers ultrasound under the RCH guideline. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: DMSA scan in 4-6 months to assess for renal scarring as the proposed approach
options.rationale: DMSA is part of the NICE pathway, not the RCH cascade. The RCH guideline does not routinely use DMSA after first typical UTI. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Micturating cystourethrogram to exclude vesicoureteric reflux
options.rationale: MCUG is not part of the RCH cascade after a single typical UTI. It is reserved for recurrent UTI with abnormal ultrasound, or atypical features such as poor response to antibiotics or a non-E coli organism. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Renal tract ultrasound during this admission as the proposed approach
options.rationale: RCH recommends renal tract ultrasound during the index admission for infants under 6 months with their first febrile UTI, OR when antenatal monitoring is not confirmed reliable. This child's antenatal scans are reported as normal but the under-6-month age alone meets the imaging criterion. Ultrasound assesses anatomy and excludes hydronephrosis or obstruction. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Cystoscopy and posterior urethral valve assessment under urology
options.rationale: Cystoscopy is reserved for suspected posterior urethral valves in a male infant with antenatal hydronephrosis, poor stream, or palpable bladder. With normal antenatal scans and a typical UTI presentation this is not indicated. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: The RCH UTI imaging cascade recommends renal tract ultrasound after a first febrile UTI in infants under 6 months. DMSA and MCUG are NICE constructs and not part of the RCH cascade for typical first UTI. Further imaging is added only for atypical features (poor response, non-E coli, abnormal ultrasound) or recurrent UTI. This case meets the under-6-month criterion regardless of antenatal scan history.
key_points: Under 6 months + first febrile UTI = ultrasound
DMSA and MCUG are not in the RCH cascade after typical UTI
Atypical features (non-E coli, poor response, mass, raised creatinine) escalate imaging
Recurrent UTI with abnormal US triggers MCUG
explanation.pearls: RCH imaging cascade differs from NICE: AU practice uses ultrasound as the index investigation and reserves DMSA/MCUG for atypical or recurrent disease, not first typical UTI.

stem:
A 6-year-old boy is reviewed in the paediatric clinic 3 weeks after diagnosis of IgA vasculitis (Henoch-Schonlein purpura). At diagnosis he had palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower limbs, arthralgia of the ankles, and one episode of colicky abdominal pain that settled with paracetamol. His baseline urinalysis was clear and renal function normal.

Today his rash has faded, the arthralgia has resolved, and he is back at school. On routine follow-up urinalysis he has 2+ protein and 1+ blood. His blood pressure today is 116/76 mmHg, on the 95th centile for age and height.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 92, BP 116/76 mmHg (95th centile for age/height), RR 18, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Fading purpura over lower limbs, no joint swelling, no abdominal tenderness, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis protein 2+, blood 1+; urine protein:creatinine ratio 88 mg/mmol (<25); creatinine 42 micromol/L (28-66), albumin 36 g/L (35-50), urine MC&S no growth

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure and continue weekly urinalysis with routine GP follow-up
options.rationale: A urine protein:creatinine ratio of 88 mg/mmol is well above the nephrotic-range threshold of 250 and significantly above the upper limit of normal of 25; this level of proteinuria with co-incident hypertension at the 95th centile triggers paediatric nephrology referral, not reassurance. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 4 weeks as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Steroids in HSP are used for severe abdominal pain or arthritis and do not prevent or reliably treat HSP nephritis; treatment decisions for HSP nephritis follow renal biopsy histology and are guided by paediatric nephrology. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Refer to paediatric nephrology for assessment within 1-2 weeks
options.rationale: RCH HSP follow-up triggers nephrology referral for any of: persistent proteinuria (uPCR over 25 mg/mmol beyond 4 weeks), hypertension, raised creatinine, or nephrotic syndrome. This child has proteinuria approaching nephrotic range with hypertension at the 95th centile and needs early renal biopsy planning. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Start oral perindopril 1 mg daily and review BP in 4 weeks
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are commonly used in HSP nephritis with persistent proteinuria but the choice and dosing should be guided by a paediatric nephrologist after histology, not initiated in GP follow-up without specialist input. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange 24-hour urinary protein collection before any specialist referral
options.rationale: A spot urine protein:creatinine ratio is the recommended quantification in paediatric practice and 24-hour collection is impractical in young children. Specialist referral should not wait on additional confirmatory testing. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: HSP renal involvement appears within 6 months of presentation in up to 40% of children and is the main determinant of long-term outcome. RCH triggers paediatric nephrology referral for persistent proteinuria, hypertension, raised creatinine, or nephrotic syndrome. Steroids do not prevent HSP nephritis. Antihypertensives and ACE inhibitors are initiated by specialists after biopsy assessment.
key_points: HSP renal monitoring with serial urinalysis and BP for at least 6 months
Trigger for nephrology: persistent proteinuria, hypertension, raised creatinine
uPCR over 25 mg/mmol is significant; over 250 mg/mmol is nephrotic range
Steroids do not prevent or reliably treat HSP nephritis
explanation.pearls: HSP nephritis severity is dictated by biopsy histology, not clinical features alone; AU practice escalates early to paediatric nephrology when proteinuria with hypertension co-exist.

stem:
A 5-year-old girl is brought to your GP clinic by her mother with 3 weeks of perianal and vulval itch worse at night, with occasional white-yellow vaginal discharge. Her mother has noted small white worms on the perianal skin when applying nappy cream. Her 3-year-old brother has been scratching his bottom for the last week. The girl is otherwise well, fully immunised, and attends preschool.

On examination there is mild erythema around the perianal area and vulva, with a small amount of non-offensive discharge. Bedside microscopy of a perianal tape sample shows oval eggs flattened on one side.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 96, BP 94/58 mmHg, RR 22, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild perianal and vulval erythema, scant non-offensive discharge, no excoriation breaks, no anal fissure, abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Perianal tape: oval eggs flattened on one side. Vulval swab: mixed normal flora, no Candida, no STI organisms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Single dose oral mebendazole 100 mg now to the girl only, with a repeat dose in 2 weeks as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Treating only the index child misses the household reservoir; reinfection and ongoing symptoms are highly likely without simultaneous family treatment. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Refer to paediatric gynaecology for assessment of vulvovaginitis and possible sexual abuse as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Threadworm is a common cause of childhood vulvovaginitis with characteristic itch and discharge. There is no clinical feature here triggering child protection referral; explicit features (recurrent unexplained STI, disclosure, anogenital injury) would prompt that pathway. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Topical clotrimazole cream twice daily to the vulva and perianal area for 7 days as the proposed approach
options.rationale: The vulval swab excludes candidiasis and the perianal tape confirms enterobiasis. Antifungal therapy does not treat threadworm. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Single dose oral mebendazole 100 mg now and repeated at 2 weeks for all household members aged 6 months and over, with hygiene advice
options.rationale: AMH recommends treating the index case and all household members aged 6 months and older with mebendazole 100 mg single dose repeated at 2 weeks to interrupt the egg-laying cycle. Hygiene measures (nail trimming, daily morning shower, hot wash of bed linen and underwear) are essential adjuncts. The repeat dose targets larvae that mature after the first dose. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

options.text: Single dose oral albendazole 400 mg once with no repeat and no household treatment as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Albendazole is an alternative agent but a single dose without repeat allows larvae to mature and re-establish infection; the repeat at 2 weeks is essential. Treating the household is also required. (Source: AMH Children's Dosing Companion)

explanation.summary: Threadworm (Enterobius vermicularis) is a common cause of perianal itch and secondary vulvovaginitis in girls. AU practice treats with oral mebendazole 100 mg single dose repeated at 2 weeks for the index case and all household members aged 6 months and over, plus hygiene measures. Topical antifungals do not treat threadworm. Vulvovaginitis from threadworm does not warrant protection referral without explicit suspicious features.
key_points: Tape test eggs are pathognomonic for Enterobius vermicularis
Mebendazole 100 mg single dose + repeat at 2 weeks; treat household
Hygiene: nail trimming, morning shower, hot wash linen
Vulvovaginitis is a common secondary feature in girls
explanation.pearls: The repeat dose at 2 weeks targets larvae that mature after the first dose (mebendazole has no larvicidal effect on eggs in utero); skipping it is the commonest treatment failure.

stem:
A 13-year-old girl with established type 1 diabetes is brought in by ambulance with 18 hours of vomiting and abdominal pain. She has been managing on a basal-bolus regimen with continuous glucose monitoring. Her usual home blood sugar logs have been stable until 3 days ago when she stopped eating and self-reduced her insulin.

In the resuscitation bay she is drowsy with Kussmaul respiration. Her abdomen is tender in the right iliac fossa with rebound and guarding. Bedside glucose 24 mmol/L, blood ketones 6.2 mmol/L. She is started on the RCH DKA pathway with 10 mL/kg saline bolus, fluid replacement, and an insulin infusion at 0.05 unit/kg/hour after 1 hour. Two hours into therapy her ketones are 4.8 mmol/L and her abdominal tenderness has not improved.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 138, BP 102/68 mmHg, RR 36 Kussmaul, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 37.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy GCS 13, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 seconds, right iliac fossa tenderness with rebound and guarding, bowel sounds reduced
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 24 mmol/L, ketones 6.2 mmol/L; venous gas pH 7.10, HCO3 6 mmol/L, base deficit -22, sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 4.6 mmol/L, chloride 102 mmol/L; lipase 38 U/L (<60); white cell count 22 x10^9/L (5-15); abdominal ultrasound: dilated non-compressible appendix 9 mm with surrounding fluid
data_table.Medications: DKA pathway: maintenance + deficit replacement saline with KCl, insulin infusion 0.05 unit/kg/hour

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Discuss urgent surgical review and theatre once initial fluid and potassium resuscitation is established, continuing the insulin infusion intraoperatively
options.rationale: The ultrasound is diagnostic for appendicitis and the abdominal signs are not explained by DKA alone (no lipase rise, focal RIF rebound persisting after fluid resuscitation, leucocytosis above DKA baseline). RCH and ANZCA paediatric peri-operative DKA guidance support proceeding to theatre once circulation is restored and potassium is replacing, with continuous insulin infusion plus glucose-containing fluid to prevent hypoglycaemia. Source control is the priority; pH need not normalise pre-operatively. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Continue DKA pathway and reassess the abdomen once acidosis fully resolves before any surgical decision as the proposed approach
options.rationale: The ultrasound finding of a non-compressible 9 mm appendix with surrounding fluid is diagnostic of acute appendicitis; persisting tenderness with rebound and guarding despite 2 hours of resuscitation, plus a white cell count of 22 and normal lipase, points to true surgical pathology rather than ketoacidosis-related abdominal pain. Delay until DKA resolution risks perforation. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Stop the insulin infusion to allow ketones to clear before surgical review as the proposed approach with the proposed clinical action
options.rationale: Stopping insulin worsens ketoacidosis and is contraindicated; insulin infusion is continued through and after surgery in known DKA. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Give intravenous tramadol and morphine and trial conservative management with intravenous antibiotics alone as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Non-operative management of paediatric appendicitis is investigational and not recommended in a child with established DKA, drowsiness, and ultrasound evidence of complicated appendicitis; surgery is the primary management. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

options.text: Arrange immediate CT abdomen with contrast to confirm appendicitis before any surgical referral as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Ultrasound has already confirmed appendicitis in this child; CT adds ionising radiation and contrast load in established DKA without changing the management decision. Paediatric appendicitis imaging is ultrasound-first in AU practice. (Source: RCH Melbourne CPG)

explanation.summary: DKA can cause abdominal pain that mimics surgical pathology and resolves with rehydration, but persistent focal signs with confirmatory imaging require surgical source control. The default 'rehydrate and re-examine' branch is the wrong branch when imaging is positive and signs persist. Insulin is continued through surgery and a glucose-containing fluid is added once blood sugars fall. CT is not first-line for paediatric appendicitis when ultrasound is diagnostic.
key_points: DKA can mimic acute abdomen; persistent focal signs plus positive imaging override
Restore circulation and start K+ replacement before theatre
Continue insulin infusion intra-operatively; add glucose if BSL <14 mmol/L
Ultrasound is first-line paediatric appendicitis imaging
explanation.pearls: In paediatric DKA with focal RIF signs, an ultrasound positive for appendicitis flips the textbook default - resuscitate, do not normalise pH, then proceed to theatre with insulin infusion running.

stem:
A 32-year-old woman in her first pregnancy attends her 28-week antenatal review. Her BMI at booking was 31 kg/m^2 and her mother has type 2 diabetes. The dating ultrasound and 20-week morphology scan were normal. She is otherwise well and on routine pregnancy vitamins.

She has returned today for the results of her 75 g oral glucose tolerance test performed at 27 weeks after an 8-hour fast. Her fasting plasma glucose is 5.4 mmol/L, 1-hour post-load 9.8 mmol/L, and 2-hour post-load 8.0 mmol/L. She has no symptoms of polyuria, polydipsia or weight loss.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78, BP 118/74 mmHg, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.6 °C
data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height 27 cm at 28 weeks, foetal heart rate 142, no oedema
data_table.Investigations: 75 g OGTT: fasting 5.4 mmol/L (<5.1), 1-hour 9.8 mmol/L (<10), 2-hour 8.0 mmol/L (<8.5)
data_table.Medications: Iodine 150 mcg, folic acid 500 mcg, vitamin D

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnosis and next step?

options.text: Normal test; reassure and continue routine antenatal care with no further glucose monitoring
options.rationale: Her fasting plasma glucose of 5.4 mmol/L exceeds the ADIPS threshold of 5.1 mmol/L for gestational diabetes; any single value above its threshold makes the diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Consensus)

options.text: Gestational diabetes; commence dietitian-led medical nutrition therapy with self-monitoring of blood glucose
options.rationale: ADIPS diagnoses GDM when any one value meets or exceeds fasting 5.1 mmol/L, 1-hour 10.0 mmol/L, or 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L; her fasting at 5.4 confirms GDM. AU first-line management is dietitian-led medical nutrition therapy plus capillary BSL monitoring (fasting and 1- or 2-hour post-prandials), with metformin or insulin added if targets are not met after 1-2 weeks. (Source: ADIPS Consensus)

options.text: Gestational diabetes; commence insulin glargine 10 units nocte immediately as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is added when lifestyle measures do not achieve glycaemic targets (fasting under 5.0, 1-hour under 7.4, or 2-hour under 6.7 mmol/L) after a 1-2 week trial, not at diagnosis. (Source: ADIPS Consensus)

options.text: Type 2 diabetes overt in pregnancy; commence insulin and refer to endocrinology urgently
options.rationale: Overt diabetes in pregnancy is diagnosed by fasting plasma glucose at or above 7.0 mmol/L, random glucose at or above 11.1 mmol/L, or HbA1c at or above 6.5%. Her fasting of 5.4 mmol/L meets GDM criteria only. (Source: ADIPS Consensus)

options.text: Borderline result; repeat the OGTT in 2 weeks before any diagnostic label as the proposed approach
options.rationale: ADIPS does not recommend repeating an OGTT when a single value crosses a threshold; the diagnosis stands and management starts. (Source: ADIPS Consensus)

explanation.summary: ADIPS uses fasting 5.1 / 1-hour 10.0 / 2-hour 8.5 mmol/L thresholds; any single value at or above its threshold diagnoses GDM. First-line is dietitian-led medical nutrition therapy plus capillary BSL monitoring, with pharmacotherapy added after 1-2 weeks if targets are not met. Targets are fasting under 5.0, 1-hour under 7.4, or 2-hour under 6.7 mmol/L. Overt diabetes in pregnancy uses different thresholds.
key_points: ADIPS OGTT thresholds: 5.1 / 10.0 / 8.5 mmol/L
Any single value at threshold diagnoses GDM
First-line: medical nutrition therapy + capillary BSL monitoring
Targets: fasting <5.0, 1-hour <7.4, 2-hour <6.7 mmol/L
explanation.pearls: ADIPS thresholds are lower and more sensitive than older 2-step approaches; AU practice catches more milder GDM and manages most cases without pharmacotherapy.

stem:
A 24-year-old woman attends your GP clinic to discuss contraception. She has been on a combined oral contraceptive pill (ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg / levonorgestrel 150 mcg) for the past 2 years with good cycle control. Over the last 6 months she has developed two episodes of unilateral visual scintillations lasting 20 minutes followed by a unilateral throbbing headache; her GP previously documented migraine without aura.

She smokes 3 cigarettes daily, BMI is 24 kg/m^2, blood pressure today is 122/78 mmHg, and her family history is unremarkable. She is sexually active in a monogamous relationship and wants long-term reliable contraception with low maintenance.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72, BP 122/78 mmHg, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, temperature 36.5 °C
data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular and neurological examination normal, no carotid bruit, no visual field defect
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening up to date and normal; routine bloods within normal limits 6 months ago
data_table.Medications: Combined oral contraceptive ethinyloestradiol 30 mcg / levonorgestrel 150 mcg daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in contraception?

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel intrauterine system after counselling on insertion and bleeding patterns
options.rationale: Migraine with aura makes combined hormonal contraception UKMEC 4 and progestogen-only options become preferred. A 52 mg levonorgestrel IUS is UKMEC 1 in migraine with aura, provides 5-8 years of highly effective contraception, and aligns with her preference for long-term reliable low-maintenance contraception. (Source: UKMEC)

options.text: Switch to a 20 mcg ethinyloestradiol combined pill and review symptoms in 3 months
options.rationale: Migraine with aura at any age is a UKMEC category 4 (absolute contraindication) for combined hormonal contraception regardless of oestrogen dose because of the ischaemic stroke risk; switching to a lower-dose combined pill does not mitigate this. (Source: UKMEC)

options.text: Continue current pill and add aspirin 100 mg daily for stroke prophylaxis as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Aspirin does not reduce the oestrogen-related stroke risk in migraine with aura to acceptable levels and adds bleeding risk; contraindicated hormonal use should be ceased, not pharmacologically counterbalanced. (Source: UKMEC)

options.text: Switch to the vaginal ring (etonogestrel/ethinyloestradiol) and review in 3 months
options.rationale: The vaginal ring is a combined oestrogen-progestogen method and carries the same UKMEC 4 contraindication in migraine with aura. (Source: UKMEC)

options.text: Recommend condoms alone with cessation of all hormonal contraception as the proposed approach
options.rationale: Condoms are user-dependent with typical-use failure around 13% per year and do not meet her stated preference for long-term reliable low-maintenance contraception; progestogen-only LARC is preferable. (Source: FSRH)

explanation.summary: Migraine with aura at any age is UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception because of ischaemic stroke risk. Progestogen-only methods (levonorgestrel IUS, etonogestrel implant, depot, progestogen-only pill) are all safe in this scenario. A 52 mg LNG-IUS is UKMEC 1 with 5-8 years of high-efficacy contraception that aligns with this patient's stated preference. Lower-dose combined pills, the ring, and adjunctive aspirin do not solve the contraindication.
key_points: Migraine with aura = UKMEC 4 for combined hormonal contraception
Progestogen-only methods are UKMEC 1-2
LNG-IUS = 5-8 years, highly effective, low maintenance
Stroke risk drives the contraindication, not headache itself
explanation.pearls: Migraine with aura roughly doubles stroke risk; adding combined hormonal contraception multiplies the risk further. The aura - not the headache - is the discriminator triggering UKMEC 4.
